title: The First Day of Thai Is the Tamil New Year Festival!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The translation preserves the printed historical claims, attributed statements and verse quotations. Visually confirmed OCR defects are repaired only in the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: In the announcement made in his address to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 23 January 2008, the Honourable Governor stated that all Tamil scholars had unanimously accepted as fact that the Tamil New Year begins on the first day of Thai, when the Tiruvalluvar year commences. The DMK government had therefore decided to declare and implement the first day of Thai as the beginning of the Tamil New Year. He further said that the people of Tamil Nadu, who already revered and celebrated Pongal as the festival of the Tamil people, would henceforth combine it with the birth of the Tamil New Year and celebrate the day with doubled joy and resurgence: planting the three fruit trees, banana, mango and jackfruit; drawing multicoloured kolams; making homes glow with rows of lamps and display new beauty; wearing new clothes; singing and dancing in celebration of Tamil dignity and self-respect; spreading the spirit of equality; and showering affection in the bounding joy that this was the Tamil New Year.

In 1939, the All India Tamils Conference met in Tiruchi under the leadership of Somasundara Bharathiar. Thanthai Periyar, Karanthai Tamil Sangam president Umamaheswaranar, Maraimalai Adigal, revolutionary poet Bharathidasan, Tamil Thendral Thiru. Vi. Ka., Professor K. Subramaniam, T.P. Meenakshisundaranar, Tamilavel P.T. Rajan, Arcot Ramasamy Mudaliar, Pattukkottai Alagiri and many others participated. That conference, too, declared that the first day of Thai was the Tamil New Year and Pongal the festival of the Tamil people.

Dravidar Kazhagam president and Tamil leader Ilaval Veeramani wrote: “Our longstanding demand that the first day of Thai mark the beginning of the Tamil New Year has succeeded. This is a new chapter of renaissance in the cultural history of the Tamil people. We commend it and rejoice.”

The day after the Governor's announcement, Thavathiru Kundrakudi Ponnambala Adigalar said in a statement: “From ancient times, in relation to religion, natural life, the soil and its people, and agricultural life, the first day of Thai has been a festival that brings resurgence and joy into Tamil life. Our thanks and appreciation go to Muthamizh Arignar and Chief Minister Kalaignar for implementing what eminent senior Tamil scholars such as Maraimalai Adigal declared: that the first day of Thai should begin the Tamil New Year in the name of Ayyan Tiruvalluvar.”

Kaviperarasu Vairamuthu wrote: “Sentient Tamils celebrate and dance with joy at the Kalaignar government's announcement in the Governor's Address that the first day of Thai marks the beginning of the Tamil New Year. Every nationality possesses certain identities that cannot be erased. The Tamil people have a territorial identity and an ethnic identity, but their identity in time alone remained confused. That darkness of confusion has now broken and dawned into light. The Dravidian movement presented Ayyan Tiruvalluvar as the measure of the world of thought. Today the Kalaignar government has caused Tiruvalluvar to be regarded as the measure of time as well. This is history that corrects history.”

This beneficial initiative enacted under the DMK government later faced grave danger under AIADMK rule. In Tamil Nadu's history, there has always existed a reactionary group that is hostile to Tamil itself, that detests, excludes and neglects anything called “classical Tamil.” Even in ancient times, there were one or two scholars who hated Tamil in this manner. Here is a story.

In Nakkiran's time, a man named Kuyakkondan reportedly declared at a debate in the Tamil Sangam, “Aryam is good; Tamil is bad.” Nakkiran immediately grew angry and sang a moral imprecation: “You who said Tamil is bad and the northern tongue is good, may you die.” Kuyakkondan at once fell down dead. Some of those present then asked Nakkiran to restore him to life. Hearing them, Nakkiran sang:
“For saying Aryam is good and Tamil is bad, he was seized by Death; by the command of Agastya of noble Podhigai, may refined Tamil restore him, svaha!”
Kuyakkondan is said to have returned to life.
Following the announcement in the Governor's Address, on 29 January 2008 I introduced the Tamil New Year Bill in the Assembly.

The Bill was debated on 1 February 2008. E.S.S. Raman for the Congress, K. Arumugam for the PMK, N. Nanmaran for the Communist Party of India (Marxist), Vai. Sivapunniyam for the Communist Party of India, M. Kannappan for the MDMK and Ku. Selvam for the Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi all welcomed it wholeheartedly, and it was passed.

Tamils in Malaysia celebrate the first day of Thai as New Year. Dr Mu. Varadarasanar explained: “In former times, the first day of Chithirai was not the beginning of the year. The elders celebrated the first day of Thai as the year's beginning. On that day, they bought and wore new clothes. They laid fresh earth and red soil in the streets and put them in order. Festivals were held throughout the town. In this manner, Pongal is celebrated in the cities as the birth of the New Year.”

A decision praised and celebrated in all these ways was enacted as law under the DMK government and implemented from 2008. But under the AIADMK government, a Bill was introduced in the Assembly to abolish it in anger, apparently because something enacted under DMK rule could not be allowed to remain. Reporters told me that both Communist parties opposed the Bill in the House and asked for my view. I replied, “I bow to their Tamil feeling.”

PMK founder Dr Ramadoss said in a statement that the AIADMK government's decision would cause anguish to those with Tamil feeling, that suddenly shifting the Tamil New Year back to Chithirai was improper, and that it would become a great historical error. Tamil leader K. Veeramani also strongly condemned the decision in comments to reporters.
Indeed, I wish to recall that revolutionary poet Bharathidasan himself sang:
“Tamil, lying asleep! Chithirai is not your Tamil New Year. For the Tamil who ruled the earth, the first day of Thai is the Tamil New Year!”

Under the DMK government, we made every arrangement enthusiastically and splendidly to celebrate the first day of Thai as both the festival of the Tamil people and the Tamil New Year. But when the AIADMK came to power in 2011, just as it held closing ceremonies for the DMK government's other schemes out of intense resentment, it reversed the first day of Thai as Tamil New Year, introduced a Bill declaring instead that the first day of Chithirai was the Tamil New Year, and passed it in the Assembly.

Did this humiliate the DMK or its government? No. Can the AIADMK government's distortionist Bill falsify the ancient history of the first day of Thai as the Tamil New Year? For our part, we celebrate Pongal as the “festival of the Tamil people,” and we shall continue to do so. Just as the English celebrate the first day of their year, we too must hold a festival and celebrate the first day of ours. Let the enemies of the Tamil people do whatever they please.

For us, and for all Tamils of genuine feeling, the first day of Thai is the day the Tamil New Year begins. At the same time, as I have said before, we shall not prevent those who wish to celebrate on the first day of Chithirai. For our part, we shall celebrate the first day of Thai as the Tamil New Year. On that day, let us hang electric lights like festoons everywhere, suspend lights from every tree so that they twinkle like fireflies, decorate every shopfront attractively, adorn every home with beauty, and celebrate the day with distinction. Without distinction between rich and poor, let everyone dress in new clothes and hold the festival with renewed spirit, spreading the fragrance of equality on this festival of the Tamil people. Let us establish before the world that the first day of Thai is the Tamil New Year festival!
Affectionately,
M.K.
11 January 2014
